expanded_description: Selecting the best title or claim for a passage is a synthesis skill that tests whether a student has grasped the central idea and can distinguish it from partial, tangential, or distorted alternatives. On the SAT, this skill appears in questions that ask students to choose the title that best captures the passage's content or the claim that most accurately represents the author's argument. These questions require the student to hold the entire passage in mind, evaluate each option against the full text, and reject choices that are too narrow, too broad, misleading, or unsupported. Mastery of this skill demonstrates that the student has moved beyond comprehending individual parts to understanding the passage as a unified whole.

core_concept.principle:
A title or claim is an ultra-condensed summary of the passage's central idea. It must capture the essential argument, subject, or purpose of the entire text in a phrase or single sentence. The best title or claim is the one that most completely and accurately represents what the passage is about and what the author is arguing, without introducing information not present in the text and without omitting any major dimension of the argument.

Selecting the best title or claim involves a two-part evaluation process:

(1) The Coverage Test: Does the candidate title or claim encompass the entire passage? A title that only reflects one paragraph or one line of argument is too narrow. A title that could apply to many passages on the same general topic is too broad. The correct choice hits the precise scope of the passage—neither more nor less than what the author actually covers.

(2) The Accuracy Test: Does the candidate title or claim faithfully represent the author's argument without distortion? A title that misstates the author's position, exaggerates a tentative claim into an absolute one, or introduces a value judgment the author never made is inaccurate, regardless of how appealing or memorable it may be.

These two tests—coverage and accuracy—must be applied to every candidate choice. A choice that passes one test but fails the other is incorrect.
core_concept.explanation:
Choosing a title is like naming a painting. The title must reflect what the painting actually depicts, not what the viewer wishes it depicted or what one small corner of the canvas shows. If a painting shows a stormy seascape with a single ship struggling against the waves, the title 'The Storm' is accurate but too broad—it could describe any storm. The title 'The Ship's Mast' is too narrow—it focuses on one detail. The title 'A Peaceful Harbor' is simply wrong. The best title might be 'The Struggle Against the Gale'—it captures the subject, the action, and the emotional tone, all in one phrase.

On the SAT, title and claim questions are particularly revealing because they test comprehensive understanding. A student who has only a vague sense of what the passage is about will gravitate toward the too-broad or too-narrow options—the choices that feel familiar because they contain words from the passage. A student who has truly synthesized the argument will recognize that these choices are incomplete or imprecise and will select the choice that accurately captures the whole.

The distinction between a title and a claim is worth noting. A title is typically a phrase or fragment that names the passage's subject and indicates its angle. A claim is a complete declarative sentence that states the author's central argument. The same principles of coverage and accuracy apply to both. The primary difference is that a claim must include the author's specific thesis, while a title may sometimes indicate the topic and approach without stating the full argument. However, on the SAT, even title questions effectively require students to identify the full argument, because the incorrect title options almost always misrepresent the passage's scope or position.

image.alt: A target diagram with a bullseye labeled 'Best Title/Claim,' surrounded by rings labeled 'Too Broad,' 'Too Narrow,' 'Distorted,' and 'Unsupported,' visually representing the Coverage and Accuracy tests
image.caption: Selecting the best title or claim requires hitting the bullseye: the choice that is neither too broad nor too narrow, and completely accurate

sections.section_title: Foundation: The Two Tests for Evaluating Titles and Claims
sections.introduction: Before students can select the best title or claim from among multiple options, they must internalize the two evaluative tests—Coverage and Accuracy—and learn to recognize the specific ways in which incorrect choices fail these tests. This section defines each test and catalogs the common failure patterns that appear in SAT answer choices.

key_concept.definition:
The Coverage Test evaluates whether a candidate title or claim encompasses the entire passage. A choice fails the Coverage Test if it is too narrow (capturing only a part of the passage), too broad (capturing a general topic that extends beyond what the passage actually discusses), or misweighted (emphasizing a minor element while ignoring a major one).

The Accuracy Test evaluates whether a candidate title or claim faithfully represents the author's argument. A choice fails the Accuracy Test if it distorts the author's position (exaggerating, softening, or reversing the claim), introduces information or judgments not present in the passage, or attributes to the author a view the author does not hold or is merely reporting from another source.

Every incorrect title or claim choice on the SAT fails one or both of these tests. The student's task is to diagnose the specific failure in each incorrect choice, which simultaneously confirms why the correct choice is correct.
key_concept.context: On the SAT, title and claim questions are often among the last questions for a passage, testing cumulative understanding. Students who have done the work of identifying the central idea as they read will find these questions straightforward. Students who have read passively, absorbing details without synthesizing an argument, will be vulnerable to choices that pass one test but fail the other.

categories.description: The ways in which candidate titles or claims fail to encompass the full scope of the passage.

examples.explanation:
The candidate choice accurately reflects one paragraph, one example, or one line of argument from the passage but ignores the rest. It treats a part as if it were the whole.

Example: A passage discusses three major causes of the French Revolution—economic crisis, Enlightenment ideas, and political mismanagement. A candidate title, 'The Economic Origins of the French Revolution,' is too narrow. It captures only the first cause and ignores the other two. A student who remembers the vivid economic statistics but glossed over the other sections might find this choice appealing. The coverage test reveals that it only fits one-third of the passage.

The candidate choice identifies the general subject of the passage but says nothing about the author's specific angle or argument. It could serve as the title of any passage on that subject.

Example: A passage argues specifically that the French Revolution's most lasting legacy was the establishment of secular nationalism as a political force. A candidate title, 'The French Revolution,' is too broad. It names the topic but communicates nothing about the author's thesis. A reader who has only identified the topic and not the argument will see this choice and think, 'Yes, the passage is about the French Revolution,' without realizing that a title must reflect what the passage says about the French Revolution.

The candidate choice emphasizes a detail that, while present in the passage, plays only a minor or incidental role in the overall argument. This is subtler than the too-narrow error because the choice may mention elements from across the passage while still misrepresenting what the passage is primarily about.

Example: A passage argues that the French Revolution's violence, while horrifying, was instrumental in breaking the hold of feudal institutions that peaceful reform had failed to dislodge. A candidate title, 'The Guillotine: Instrument of Terror in Revolutionary France,' focuses on a vivid but supporting detail. The guillotine is mentioned as an example of the violence, but the passage is about the political function of that violence, not about the guillotine itself. The title misweights a minor detail into the apparent focus.

categories.description: The ways in which candidate titles or claims misrepresent the author's actual argument.

examples.process: Distortion: The Exaggeration or Reversal Error
examples.explanation:
The candidate choice takes the author's actual argument and pushes it to an extreme, softens it into a weaker claim, or reverses its direction entirely. This often involves changing modal verbs ('may' becomes 'will'), removing qualifications, or inserting absolute language.

Example: An author argues that 'the available evidence suggests a possible link between diet and cognitive decline, though the causal mechanism remains unclear.' A candidate claim states: 'The author argues that poor diet is the primary cause of cognitive decline in aging populations.' This is a distortion. The author's tentative 'suggests a possible link' becomes the definitive 'is the primary cause.' The qualifying 'causal mechanism remains unclear' is erased. The distortion test catches this mismatch.

The candidate choice introduces an idea, judgment, or piece of information that is not present in the passage. This may be a plausible inference, a piece of common knowledge, or a value judgment the reader brings, but if it is not in the text, it cannot be part of a correct title or claim.

Example: A passage neutrally describes the processes by which different cultures develop unique culinary traditions. A candidate title, 'The Superiority of Mediterranean Cuisine Over Northern European Fare,' introduces a value judgment—superiority—that the passage never makes. A reader who personally prefers Mediterranean food might find this title appealing, but it is unsupported by the text.

The candidate choice attributes to the author a view that the author is actually reporting from another source—often in order to refute it. This is a common error in passages that engage with competing viewpoints.

Example: A passage begins by summarizing a critic's argument that 'public art funding is a wasteful luxury,' then proceeds to refute that argument systematically. A candidate claim states: 'The passage argues that public art funding is a wasteful luxury that should be eliminated.' This misattributes the critic's view to the author. The student must track whose voice is speaking at each point in the passage.

worked_examples.title: Evaluating Title Candidates
worked_examples.problem:
Read the following passage and then evaluate the four candidate titles. For each incorrect title, identify whether it fails the Coverage Test (too narrow, too broad, misweighted) or the Accuracy Test (distortion, unsupported, misattribution).

Passage: 'The honeybee is the world's most celebrated pollinator, but its dominance in the public imagination has come at a cost to its wild counterparts. Native bee species—bumblebees, mason bees, leafcutter bees, and thousands of others—perform pollination services that are in many cases more efficient than those of the managed honeybee. Bumblebees, for instance, are capable of buzz pollination, a vibrating technique that honeybees cannot perform and that is essential for crops like tomatoes and blueberries. Yet wild bee populations are in steep decline due to habitat loss, pesticide exposure, and competition from managed honeybee colonies that are trucked into agricultural areas. These managed colonies, far from solving the pollination crisis, may be exacerbating it by spreading disease to wild populations and monopolizing floral resources. Conservation efforts, the author argues, must shift from an exclusive focus on honeybee health to the protection of the diverse and often overlooked wild species that are equally vital to ecosystem function and agricultural productivity.'

Candidate Titles:
A) 'The Plight of the Honeybee'
B) 'Buzz Pollination: How Bumblebees Support Agriculture'
C) 'Beyond the Honeybee: The Case for Protecting Wild Pollinators'
D) 'Why Managed Honeybee Colonies Should Be Eliminated'

step_by_step.step: Step 1: Identify the Passage's Central Idea
step_by_step.explanation: Before evaluating any candidates, we establish the central idea: The passage argues that while honeybees dominate public attention, wild native bee species are equally or more important pollinators that are in decline, and that conservation efforts must shift focus from honeybees alone to include the protection of diverse wild bee populations, especially since managed honeybee colonies may actually contribute to the problem.

Candidate A: 'The Plight of the Honeybee'

Coverage Test: The honeybee is mentioned in the passage, but the passage is not about the plight of the honeybee. In fact, the honeybee is described as 'the world's most celebrated pollinator' that dominates public attention. The passage's argument is that this focus on honeybees is itself part of the problem. This title misses the entire argument about wild bees.

Diagnosis: This title fails the Coverage Test. It is too narrow in the sense that it focuses on the wrong subject entirely—it centers the honeybee, which the passage uses as a contrast point, not as the main subject. One could also argue it is a misrepresentation of the passage's actual concern.

Candidate B: 'Buzz Pollination: How Bumblebees Support Agriculture'

Coverage Test: Buzz pollination is discussed in the passage as one example of why wild bees are important. Bumblebees are one type of wild bee mentioned. But this is one specific detail within a broader argument. The passage is not primarily about buzz pollination or bumblebees specifically; it is about the need to protect diverse wild pollinators. This title treats a supporting example as the main subject.

Diagnosis: This title fails the Coverage Test. It is too narrow—it elevates one illustrative example (buzz pollination by bumblebees) into the apparent focus of the entire passage, ignoring the larger argument about conservation priorities.

Candidate C: 'Beyond the Honeybee: The Case for Protecting Wild Pollinators'

Coverage Test: This title captures the passage's central move—moving 'beyond the honeybee' to focus on wild pollinators. It encompasses both the critique of honeybee-centric attention and the argument for protecting wild species. It is neither too narrow (it covers the full argument) nor too broad (it specifies the passage's particular angle).

Accuracy Test: The title accurately reflects the author's argument—it makes a 'case for protecting' wild pollinators, which is exactly what the passage does. It does not exaggerate (it does not say 'eliminate honeybees'), it does not introduce unsupported claims, and it does not misattribute. The tone is appropriately persuasive.

Diagnosis: This title passes both tests. It is the correct choice.

Candidate D: 'Why Managed Honeybee Colonies Should Be Eliminated'

Accuracy Test: The passage argues that managed colonies may be 'exacerbating' the problem and that focus should shift to wild bees. It does not argue that managed colonies should be 'eliminated.' This is a significant distortion, turning a nuanced concern about competition and disease spread into an extreme and absolute call for elimination. The passage's argument about managed colonies is cautionary, not abolitionist.

Diagnosis: This title fails the Accuracy Test. It is a distortion—it exaggerates the author's measured concern into an extreme position the author never takes. This is a classic 'Distortion: The Exaggeration Error.'

worked_examples.final_answer: Candidate C, 'Beyond the Honeybee: The Case for Protecting Wild Pollinators,' is the correct title. Candidate A fails the Coverage Test by centering the wrong subject (the honeybee, which is the contrast point, not the focus). Candidate B fails the Coverage Test by being too narrow (focusing on one illustrative example rather than the full argument). Candidate D fails the Accuracy Test by distorting the author's nuanced concern into an extreme call for elimination.
worked_examples.concept_applied: This example demonstrates the systematic application of the Coverage Test and Accuracy Test to title selection. Each incorrect choice is diagnosed not just as 'wrong' but as failing a specific test for a specific reason, building the analytical vocabulary students need to evaluate choices on the SAT.

image.alt: A flowchart showing the evaluation process: Candidate Title -> Coverage Test (Too Narrow? Too Broad? Misweighted?) -> Accuracy Test (Distorted? Unsupported? Misattributed?) -> Correct or Incorrect
image.caption: A systematic evaluation process: each candidate title must pass both the Coverage Test and the Accuracy Test

sections.section_title: Application: Selecting Titles and Claims Across Passage Types
sections.introduction: This section provides practice with title and claim selection across the three major SAT passage types—informational, literary, and historical. For each, we will apply the Coverage and Accuracy tests and analyze how the traps differ depending on the passage genre.

key_concept.definition:
While the Coverage and Accuracy tests apply universally, the specific patterns of incorrect choices vary by passage type. Informational passage traps often involve too-narrow choices that fixate on one study or data point, or distortion choices that overstate scientific certainty. Literary passage traps frequently involve too-broad choices that state a universal theme without capturing the passage's specific treatment, or misattribution choices that take a character's belief as the story's message. Historical passage traps commonly involve misattribution of an opponent's view to the author, or distortion into extreme positions the author's nuanced argument does not support.

Recognizing these genre-specific trap patterns allows students to anticipate likely wrong answers and evaluate choices with greater precision.
key_concept.context: On the SAT, title and claim questions appear across all passage types. The skill is fundamentally the same—apply Coverage and Accuracy—but familiarity with genre conventions sharpens the student's ability to identify why a particular choice fails.

categories.description: Selecting the best title for a science or social studies passage. Traps often involve too-narrow focus on a specific study or overstatement of scientific conclusions.

examples.explanation:
Passage Summary: A social science passage reviews three longitudinal studies on the effects of bilingualism on cognitive aging. Two studies find that bilingual individuals develop dementia symptoms four to five years later than monolingual individuals. A third study finds no significant difference. The author discusses possible methodological explanations for the discrepancy—differences in how bilingualism is defined, variations in controlling for education and socioeconomic status—and concludes that while the evidence is not unanimous, the preponderance of data suggests bilingualism provides a cognitive reserve that delays dementia symptoms, though the precise mechanism remains unknown.

Correct Title: 'Bilingualism and Cognitive Aging: Evidence for a Protective Effect, with Caveats'

Coverage Analysis: This title captures the full argument—the evidence for a protective effect and the caveats about methodological discrepancies and unknown mechanisms. It is neither too narrow (it does not focus on one study) nor too broad (it specifies the passage's particular conclusion).

Accuracy Analysis: The title's hedging ('evidence for,' 'with caveats') matches the author's measured tone. A trap title like 'Bilingualism Proven to Prevent Dementia' would fail the Accuracy Test by distorting a tentative, preponderance-of-evidence conclusion into a definitive, absolute claim.

categories.description: Selecting the best title for a fiction or literary nonfiction passage. Traps often involve too-broad thematic statements or elevation of a minor symbol to the apparent focus.

examples.explanation:
Passage Summary: A first-person narrative describes a woman's visit to her childhood home, now abandoned and decaying. She walks through the empty rooms, recalling specific moments of family life that occurred in each space. A recurring motif is the sound of a grandfather clock that no longer works. The narrative voice is elegiac, reflecting on the relationship between physical spaces and the memories they hold, and concluding with the realization that the house will be demolished soon, erasing the physical container of her childhood. The passage is not primarily about loss in general but about the specific experience of confronting the imminent physical erasure of a memory-laden place.

Correct Title: 'The Last Visit: Memory and the Doomed House'

Coverage Analysis: This title captures both the narrative situation (the last visit) and the thematic concern (memory and the doomed house). It is specific to this passage's treatment of the theme.

Accuracy Analysis: A trap title like 'The Grandfather Clock: A Story of Lost Time' would fail the Coverage Test as misweighted—it elevates one symbolic object to the apparent focus, even though the clock is a supporting motif, not the primary subject. A trap title like 'The Inevitability of Loss in Human Life' would fail the Coverage Test as too broad—it states a universal theme without capturing the passage's specific narrative situation.

categories.description: Selecting the best claim for a historical document. Traps often involve misattribution of an opponent's view or distortion through modern value judgments.

examples.explanation:
Passage Summary: A nineteenth-century essay argues for the expansion of public libraries. The author acknowledges the objection that public funding for libraries is an improper use of tax money that benefits only the leisure class. The author refutes this by arguing that libraries are not luxuries but essential institutions for democratic self-governance, enabling citizens of all classes to inform themselves on public issues. The author further argues that libraries serve as a ladder of social mobility for the industrious poor. The essay's final paragraph envisions a nation in which every town has a free public library as a 'bulwark of liberty' against the forces of ignorance and demagoguery.

Correct Claim: 'The author argues that public libraries are not a luxury for the leisure class but an essential democratic institution that enables informed citizenship and social mobility, and should be expanded accordingly.'

Coverage Analysis: This claim encompasses both the refutation of the opposing view and the positive arguments for libraries. It covers the full argumentative arc.

Accuracy Analysis: A trap claim like 'The author argues that public libraries are a wasteful use of tax money that primarily benefits the wealthy' would fail the Accuracy Test through misattribution—it attributes the opponent's view to the author. A trap claim like 'The author proves that societies without public libraries inevitably collapse into tyranny' would fail the Accuracy Test through distortion—the author argues that libraries are a bulwark against ignorance, not that their absence makes tyranny inevitable.

image.alt: Three panels, each showing an annotated passage of a different type—informational, literary, historical—with correct and trap titles identified and diagnosed
image.caption: Title and claim selection requires applying Coverage and Accuracy tests across all passage types, with attention to genre-specific trap patterns

summary:
Selecting the best title or claim for a passage is a synthesis skill that requires the student to hold the entire passage in mind and evaluate candidate choices against two rigorous tests: the Coverage Test and the Accuracy Test.

The Coverage Test asks whether the candidate title or claim encompasses the full scope of the passage. A choice fails this test if it is too narrow (capturing only a paragraph, example, or single line of argument), too broad (stating only the general topic without the author's specific angle), or misweighted (emphasizing a minor detail while ignoring the central argument). The correct choice hits the precise scope of the passage—neither more nor less than what the author actually covers.

The Accuracy Test asks whether the candidate title or claim faithfully represents the author's argument without distortion. A choice fails this test if it distorts the author's position (exaggerating a tentative claim, softening a strong one, or reversing the argument's direction), introduces information or value judgments not present in the passage (unsupported claims), or misattributes to the author a view the author is merely reporting or refuting (the wrong-voice error). The correct choice captures the author's actual argument as stated, not as the reader might expect or prefer it to be.

The specific patterns of incorrect choices vary by passage genre. Informational passage traps often present too-narrow choices that fixate on a single study or data point, or distortion choices that turn scientific caution into definitive proof. Literary passage traps frequently offer too-broad thematic statements that lack the passage's specific narrative treatment, or misweighted choices that elevate a minor symbol to the apparent focus. Historical passage traps commonly involve misattribution of an opponent's view to the author, or distortion of a nuanced political argument into an extreme position.

The evaluation process is systematic. For each candidate choice, the student must ask:

(1) Does this title or claim cover everything important in the passage? (Coverage Test)

(2) Does this title or claim accurately represent what the author actually argues, without adding, subtracting, or changing anything? (Accuracy Test)

A choice that passes both tests is the correct answer. A choice that fails either test, for any reason, is incorrect. The discipline of diagnosing why each incorrect choice fails—is it too narrow? too broad? distorted? unsupported? misattributed?—builds the analytical precision that the SAT rewards and that constitutes genuine critical reading skill. The best title or claim is not the one that sounds most impressive or the one that contains the most words from the passage, but the one that most accurately and completely captures what the passage is actually about and what the author is actually arguing.
